uncertainty_flags: Obtain written confirmation (fax receipt, portal submission record) of the December 1 renewal application — this timestamp is the cornerstone of the appeal
Confirm HealthFirst's stated turnaround standard in writing (plan documents or member handbook) to cite it formally in the appeal
denial_reason_identified: HealthFirst denied December 16–31 claims because the prior authorization expired December 15 and no renewal was on file — but the renewal was submitted timely on December 1 and HealthFirst failed to process it before the expiration date.
appeal_grounds: BronxCare submitted the renewal authorization request on December 1, 2024 — 14 days before the expiration date — consistent with HealthFirst's own stated 15-day advance submission requirement; the provider met its obligation

HealthFirst took 36 days to issue a determination on the renewal request, well beyond its standard 14-day processing window for non-urgent prior authorization requests under 42 CFR 422.568; the plan's processing delay directly caused the authorization gap

CMS regulations and New York State law prohibit Medicare Advantage plans from denying medically necessary services where a timely prior authorization request was pending and the plan failed to act within required timeframes

The member's clinical condition was unchanged during December 16–31; the services rendered were identical to those previously authorized and the medical necessity was continuous, as documented by the treating physician
The subsequent approval (January 6, 2025) confirms that the services met coverage criteria — the only issue was HealthFirst's administrative processing delay, not a lack of medical necessity
recommended_attachments: Fax confirmation or portal submission record showing BronxCare's renewal request was received by HealthFirst on December 1, 2024
Copy of original authorization HF-2024-77341 (September 16 – December 15, 2024)
Copy of renewal approval issued January 6, 2025
HealthFirst member handbook or plan document stating 15-day advance submission requirement and 14-day processing standard
Physician letter of medical necessity confirming unchanged clinical condition throughout December 2024
BronxCare clinical notes for December 16–31, 2024 showing services consistent with approved care plan
appeal_letter:
Date: February 1, 2025

HealthFirst Appeals Department
P.O. Box 5100
New York, NY 10116

RE: Appeal of Claim Denial — Claim No. CLM-2025-004432
Member ID: MEM-44219
Auth No. HF-2024-77341
Service Dates: December 16–31, 2024
Provider: BronxCare Home Health Services
Denied Amount: $1,944.00

Dear HealthFirst Appeals Department,

We are writing on behalf of Member ID MEM-44219 to formally appeal the denial of Claim No. CLM-2025-004432 for home health aide services rendered December 16–31, 2024. The claim was denied on the basis that no valid prior authorization was on file for the disputed dates. We respectfully dispute this determination: the authorization gap was caused entirely by HealthFirst's failure to process a timely renewal request within the plan's own required timeframe.

BACKGROUND

The member is a 76-year-old with congestive heart failure, chronic kidney disease (Stage 3), and limited mobility following a hip replacement in August 2024. Home health aide services (4 hours/day) were authorized under Auth No. HF-2024-77341, valid September 16 – December 15, 2024.

On December 1, 2024 — 14 days before the authorization expiration — BronxCare Home Health Services submitted a renewal authorization request to HealthFirst, in compliance with the plan's stated 15-day advance submission requirement. As of December 15, 2024, HealthFirst had not issued any determination. BronxCare continued services from December 16 onward to prevent medically unsafe interruption of care for a medically fragile patient.

HealthFirst issued the renewal approval on January 6, 2025 — 36 days after receiving the application, and 22 days after the original authorization had expired.

GROUNDS FOR APPEAL

1. The provider submitted the renewal request on time. BronxCare submitted the renewal on December 1 — within the advance window required by HealthFirst policy. The provider met every obligation required to maintain continuous authorization.

2. HealthFirst failed to process the renewal within its required timeframe. Under 42 CFR 422.568, Medicare Advantage plans must issue prior authorization decisions within 14 calendar days for standard requests. HealthFirst took 36 days. The resulting authorization gap was caused by the plan's own processing delay — not by any failure of the provider or the member.

3. Federal regulations prohibit denial of covered services where an authorization request was pending and the plan failed to act in time. CMS guidance makes clear that members should not be denied medically necessary services as a result of a plan's failure to process a timely prior authorization request. Allowing HealthFirst to deny these claims would effectively penalize the member and provider for the plan's administrative failure.

4. Medical necessity was uninterrupted and confirmed. The member's clinical condition was unchanged throughout December. The services rendered December 16–31 were identical to those previously authorized. HealthFirst's own subsequent approval (January 6, 2025) confirms the services meet coverage criteria — the sole issue is HealthFirst's processing timeline.

REQUEST

We respectfully request that HealthFirst reverse the denial of Claim No. CLM-2025-004432, treat the December 1, 2024 renewal application as establishing continuous authorization through the pending determination period, and authorize payment of $1,944.00 for home health aide services rendered December 16–31, 2024.

Documentation supporting this appeal is enclosed. Please do not hesitate to contact us if additional information is required.

Sincerely,
[Authorized Representative / Care Coordinator]
Senior Center Services
[Phone] | [Email]
